fund.name: Nippon India Large Cap Fund - Direct Plan Growth Plan - Growth Option
fund.display_name: Nippon India Large Cap Fund Direct Growth
fund.fund_house: Nippon Life India Asset Management Limited

allocations.rationale: This fund invests mostly in big, stable Indian companies like Reliance and HDFC Bank. It's a solid base for your equity part since you're moderate on risk and want domestic focus after being away. Strong past performance over 5 years at 17.7% growth.

fund.name: Parag Parikh Flexi Cap Fund - Direct Plan - Growth
fund.display_name: Parag Parikh Flexi Cap Direct Growth

allocations.rationale: This fund mixes Indian stocks with some international ones (about 25-30% in global companies like Alphabet and Amazon), fitting your interest in a balanced domestic-international approach. It's flexible and has delivered steady 16.7% over 5 years, good for your long-term retirement goal.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential Equity & Debt Fund - Direct Plan - Growth
fund.display_name: ICICI Prudential Equity & Debt Direct Growth
fund.fund_house: ICICI Prudential Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: A mix of 65-80% stocks and the rest in safer bonds, this gives growth with some cushion. Taxed like equity (better for your 30% slab), and 18.9% 5-year returns make it suitable for your moderate risk and 17-year horizon to build towards 5Cr retirement.

fund.name: HSBC Equity Savings Fund - Direct Growth

allocations.rationale: This is like a safe parking spot with arbitrage (low-risk stock trades) plus some debt and equity hedge. Equity tax treatment means better post-tax returns than pure debt funds for your high tax slab. Low ups and downs, perfect for covering school fees over 8 years without much worry.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal S&P 500 Index Fund - Direct Plan Growth
fund.display_name: Motilal Oswal S&P 500 Index Direct Growth
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Tracks top 500 US companies for international diversification after your US time. Low fee at 0.2%, and 16.1% 5-year returns add global flavor to your plan without overcomplicating.

plan.setup_phase: Step 1: As an NRI returning to India, update your KYC with Aadhaar and PAN via CAMS/KFintech or apps like Groww/Kuvera (they handle NRI status). Step 2: Settle liabilities (₹12L) first from your corpus. Step 3: Open a direct mutual fund account on Groww, Kuvera, or Zerodha Coin — link your Indian bank. Step 4: Invest lumpsum ₹30L across these funds (proportional to allocations). Step 5: Set up monthly SIPs totaling ₹40,000 as specified — start right after KYC. Use growth option for all to compound returns. For school fees, redeem from the HSBC Equity Savings fund as needed over 8 years.
review_checkpoints: After 3 months: Confirm all SIPs are auto-debiting and lumpsum invested correctly.
After 1 year: Check portfolio value vs. expected (base 10% growth); review for school fee withdrawals.
Every 2 years: Assess if risk still moderate; look at returns vs. category averages (e.g., large cap should beat Nifty 50).
At age 55 (10 years in): Re-evaluate for retirement progress towards ₹5Cr.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Once a year on your birthday, check if any part (equity, hybrid, etc.) has drifted more than 5% from targets due to market moves. If equity grows to 70%, sell some and buy more hybrid/savings to reset to 60/40 split. This keeps your moderate risk in check without over-trading. Use app tools for easy swaps.
plan.rationale: At 45 with 17 years to retirement, a moderate mix of 60% growth-oriented stocks (domestic and international via Nippon, Parag Parikh, Motilal) and 40% balanced/stability (ICICI hybrid, HSBC savings) fits your caution after 10 years abroad. It aims to grow your ₹33L net corpus plus ₹40k monthly SIPs to ₹5Cr by 62, assuming 10% average growth. The international touch satisfies your US experience, while equity tax benefits beat debt post your 30% tax. For school fees, the low-risk HSBC portion provides steady access without tax drag.
risks: Stock parts can drop 20-30% in a bad year (like 2020 crash), but with 17 years, they recover — don't panic sell.
International funds like S&P 500 face US market/ currency risks (rupee weakening helps, strengthening hurts).
Hybrid and savings are steadier but still dip 5-10% in tough times; use only for fees if needed, keep emergency cash separate.
Overall, moderate setup means your total portfolio might fall 15-20% short-term but grows long-term.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

sip_step_up.description: Increase SIP by 10% every year to match expected salary/income growth and inflation, turning ₹40k/month into ~₹1.1L by year 17.

allocation_schedule.trigger: After 8 years: Shift 10% from equity to debt for school fees end and nearing 55
Retirement at 62: More safety for corpus withdrawal

aps.reasoning: Passive Instrument Fraction: Only one fund (Motilal Oswal S&P 500 Index Fund) out of three equity-based funds is passive, making passive equity exposure about 10% of total portfolio and roughly 17% of equity sleeve. Others are active funds with notable tracking errors and positive alphas. Turnover Score: Rebalancing occurs once a year with flexibility only when allocation drifts beyond 5%, indicating a mostly buy-and-hold approach with minimal tactical shifts. Cost Emphasis Score: Expense ratios around 1% for active funds and 0.2% for passive, but cost minimization is not the primary selection criteria; rationale focuses on growth, risk, and diversification. Research vs Cost Score: Fund selections show emphasis on multi-cap strategies, international exposure, and manager alpha, implying deeper research beyond just cost or index exposure. Time Horizon Alignment Score: The investor’s horizon is 17 years with long-term goals, minimal tactical trading, and encouraging patience through drawdowns, showing strong long-term alignment. Portfolio Activeness Score: Equity funds have moderate to high tracking error and positive alpha, along with varying beta, suggesting genuine active management rather than closet indexing. Hybrid and equity savings funds also show active positioning. Overall, the plan leans toward active management with some passive exposure, low-frequency rebalancing, moderate cost focus, deep research, and a long-term orientation.

pqs.reasoning: The plan is extremely well aligned with Nick's profile: it supports his moderate risk appetite and 17-year horizon with a 60/40 growth/stability split, gradually shifting to more conservative allocations by retirement. The inclusion of large cap, flexi cap (with global exposure), aggressive hybrid, equity savings, and a US index fund fits his goals, legacy holdings, and international experience well. Tax efficiency is excellent because it prioritizes equity-taxed aggressive hybrid and arbitrage/Equity Savings for the 30% slab investor, avoids pure slab-taxed debt funds, and includes tax rationale explicitly. The diversification is strong across fund houses, categories, and geographies; only minor deduction for some concentration in Nippon and ICICI and no gold fund now (planned later). Risk-return mix is appropriate with realistic 10% base return projections and clear risk warnings. The rationale, allocations, rebalancing, and tax treatment form a coherent, contradiction-free narrative. The plan also addresses his specific requests about India re-entry and school fees with suitable steps and liquidity via the HSBC Equity Savings fund.
